repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem

`vim.F` has issues as documented in #34633 and elsewhere.

## Plan

Open to changes; please comment if different things should be done.

`vim.F` is not being fully removed, as `{un,}pack_len()` will be kept.

- `vim.F.if_nil()`: Move to `vim.nonnil()`. ~~Remove in favor of `a or b or c`, `vim.iter({...}):next()`, and the new `vim.isnil()`.~~
- `vim.F.ok_or_nil()`: Remove.
- `vim.F.npcall()`: Move to `vim.npcall()`.
- `vim.F.nil_wrap()`: Remove.
- `vim.F.pack_len()`: Kept unchanged. ~~Remove.~~
- `vim.F.unpack_len()`: Kept unchanged. ~~Remove.~~
- Adds `vim.isnil()`, which tests if a value is equal to `nil` or `vim.NIL`.

Supersedes #34633

comment_data.body: for reference: these `foo_wrap` functions are telling us that people want a less verbose way to define a function. so anytime someone proposes a `foo_wrap`, that's a vote for `vim.f(foo, fn)`
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -18,36 +18,37 @@ local F = {}
 ---@param ... T
 ---@return T
 function F.if_nil(...)
-  local nargs = select('#', ...)
-  for i = 1, nargs do
-    local v = select(i, ...)
-    if v ~= nil then
-      return v
-    end
-  end
-  return nil
+  vim.deprecate('vim.F.if_nil', 'vim.nonnil', '0.14')
+  return vim.nonnil(...)
 end
 
 -- Use in combination with pcall
+--- @deprecated
 function F.ok_or_nil(status, ...)
+  vim.deprecate('vim.F.ok_or_nil', 'status and ... or nil', '0.14')
   if not status then
     return
   end
   return ...
 end
 
 -- Nil pcall.
+--- @deprecated
 --- @generic T
 --- @param fn  fun(...):T
 --- @param ... T?
 --- @return T
 function F.npcall(fn, ...)
-  return F.ok_or_nil(pcall(fn, ...))
+  vim.deprecate('vim.F.npcall', 'vim.npcall', '0.14')
+  return vim.npcall(fn, ...)
 end
 
 --- Wrap a function to return nil if it fails, otherwise the value
+--- @deprecated
 function F.nil_wrap(fn)

comment_data.body: i'm on the fence about `npcall` bc it's usually a code smell. but don't want to slow down this PR, not a blocker.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1903,6 +1915,42 @@ vim.list_slice({list}, {start}, {finish})                   *vim.list_slice()*
     Return: ~
         (`any[]`) Copy of table sliced from start to finish (inclusive)
 
+vim.nonnil({...})                                               *vim.nonnil()*
+    Returns the first argument which is not nil.
+
+    If all arguments are nil, returns nil.
+
+    Example: >lua
+        local a = nil
+        local b = nil
+        local c = 42
+        local d = true
+        assert(vim.nonnil(a, b, c, d) == 42)
+<
+
+    Attributes: ~
+        Since: 0.13.0
+
+    Parameters: ~
+      • {...}  (`any`)
+
+    Return: ~
+        (`any`)
+
+vim.npcall({fn}, {...})                                         *vim.npcall()*
+    Calls the function `fn` in `protected mode` like |pcall()|, but returns
+    `nil` on error.

comment_data.body: The instances where I saw it used in core were reasonable, at least. I agree it's not the best pattern.

comment_data.body:
should we also check `v ~= vim.NIL` here? i think that is generally what's wanted, and for cases where it's not wanted, the caller shouldn't be using convenience wrappers like this anyway? @neovim/core 

for back-compat, `vim.F.if_nil` can keep the old behavior.

p.s.: same question for `npcall`

+    local v = select(i, ...)
+    if v ~= nil then

 function F.nil_wrap(fn)

comment_data.body:
Just as a reminder, similar to `if_nil`, the purpose of this function is to address a common pitfall in Lua. Consider a table like this:
```lua
t = { 0, nil, 1 }
```
Using `unpack()` on the table above will only return `0` due to the presence of `nil`. A common pitfall is when we want to collect the arguments of a function and then pass them to another function:
```lua
function a(...)
  t = { ... }
  return function()
    b(unpack(t))
  end
end
```
If any intermediate argument of `a()` is `nil`, subsequent arguments will be lost during `unpack`. To handle this scenario, `unpack_len` is provided here along with `pack_len`, which is designed to work seamlessly with it.

+  return vim.npcall(fn, ...)
 end

   return function(...)
-    return F.npcall(fn, ...)
+    return vim.npcall(fn, ...)
   end
 end
 
+-- TODO: deprecate `F.pack_len` and `F.unpack_len`
+
 --- like {...} except preserve the length explicitly

comment_data.body: Yep, I was wrong to try to remove them in the first draft of this PR; they are still useful. I assume when they are deprecated they will just be moved somewhere else. Seems there's still discussion of where to put them though, so unlike the other functions in `F.lua` I'm not touching them in this PR (if the core team wants to make a decision right now, I could of course do that in this PR).

comment_data.body:
I'd love to get this resolved so we can get this PR merged.

> should we also check `v ~= vim.NIL` here?

It doesn't matter much either way as it'll be rare, but in my opinion I think the behavior should remain unchanged.

> p.s.: same question for `npcall`

I don't believe this applies. `npcall()` does not check whether something is nil, it just returns `nil`.

comment_data.body:
> It doesn't matter much either way as it'll be rare, but in my opinion I think the behavior should remain unchanged.

why? what is an example where a caller would *want* `vim.NIL` to be returned by `vim.nonnil` ?


actually, I can think of a use-case: `vim.nonnil` callers may want items that can be in a Lua "list".

comment_regular_data.body:
> vim.F.if_nil(): Remove in favor of a or b or c

`a or b or c` doesn't work if any value is false

The _module_ is awkward, but (most) of the functions were added for a sound reason (e.g., to work around lack of native ternary as @yochem writes or add PUC shims for LuaJIT extensions). Many of them are used in big plugins (again, for good reason there, even if the use in core is questionable), so simply removing them will be a mess. (GH search having a hissy fit doesn't help with assessing the impact of such changes, unfortunately...)

There's about 200 uses of `vim.F` in my plugin folder, mostly for `vim.F.if_nil` but also `unpack_len` (lazy.nvim) and `npcall`. That points to a definite need for `vim.F.if_nil`, so I strongly advocate for leaving it as is -- if it _has_ to be moved, it should keep the same signature and function so downstream can shim it via `ifnil = vim.ifnil or vim.F.if_nil` (which will be annoying enough).

yes we need a deprecation cycle(s) for this. 

wonder if we can find a more intuitive name for `if_nil`. From previous discussions, `nonnil` seems good to me.

Yes, choice of name is completely open (maybe something more use case-related like `vim.default_value` or somesuch?)

But to be clear: My point is that even with deprecation, we should not _remove_ these; only _move_ (and/or rename) them.

> > vim.F.if_nil(): Remove in favor of a or b or c
> 
> `a or b or c` doesn't work if any value is false

`vim.iter({...}):next()` works the exact same way, even if a value is false, which is why I chose to remove it. If people still think that `vim.F.if_nil()` is worth keeping, I can move it out of `vim.F` like I did with `npcall()`.

> also `unpack_len` (lazy.nvim)

Sounds like `unpack_len()` and `pack_len()` should be kept then. Got it.

> and `npcall`.

`npcall` is just being moved and is keeping the same exact signature.

As this is so widely used, and the replacement not at all obvious, I recommend still keeping this as a shim (with the new implementation to reduce maintenance cost). This would also make sure (through the existing test coverage) that this is _indeed_ a 1:1 replacement and does not fall apart in some edge case that wasn't considered.

Moved `vim.F.if_nil()` to `vim.nonnil()` since `vim.iter({ ... }):next()` is unintuitive. It's exactly the same, so plugin authors can do `local nonnil = vim.nonnil or vim.F.if_nil`, as suggested by @clason.

I'm also thinking it might be a good idea to not deprecate `unpack_len()` and `pack_len()` until there is a plan for them.

So `vim.F.ok_or_nil()` and `vim.F.nil_wrap()` are the only functions that will actually be removed by this PR.
I thought we decided on a soft deprecation 😠
meaning, drop the `vim.deprecate` calls, or increase the hard-deprecation version (from 0.14 )?

Meaning, drop the `vim.deprecate` call (or at least remove the warning message; checkhealth I can live with). I now have ~15 minutes to update close to 200 call sites before the inevitable deluge of issues about it...

> checkhealth I can live with

ok 👍 I think bumping the 0.14 version might do that, not sure

This PR does not implement soft deprecations.

>  - For Lua features, use `vim.deprecate()`. The specified version is the
>     current minor version + 2. For example, if the current version is
>     `v0.10.0-dev-1957+gd676746c33` then use `0.12`.

So the `vim.deprecate` calls need to have `0.15`.

I assumed 0.14 was intentional, but bumping to 0.15 also works.

(We -- with my downstream hat on -- _do_ need some way of tracking soft deprecations, though; a -- placeholder name -- `vim.deprecate.get({plugin = plugin})` one could use in CI that lists all deprecations maybe? Then we could also punt the stacktraces from the `checkhealth` to that, and `checkhealth` could simply say "use `vim.deprecate.get()` for more details".)

> We -- with my downstream hat on -- _do_ need some way of tracking soft deprecations, though; a -- placeholder name -- `vim.deprecate.get({plugin = plugin})` one could use in CI that lists all deprecations maybe

I think `:checkhealth` should get in the habit of logging its findings as diagnostics. Then plugins can simply query diagnostics. https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/36403

title: feat(lua)!: `vim.isnil`, `vim.nonnil`, deprecate `vim.F` #39495
